**Salinlahi: A 2D Pixel Art Mobile Defense Game Integrating Baybayin Character Drawing as the Core Gameplay Mechanic**

A Project Study Report  
Presented to the Faculty of the Department of Software Engineering  
Central Philippine University  
Jaro, Iloilo City, Philippines

In partial fulfillment  
Of the requirements for the Degree of  
Bachelor of Science in Software Engineering

Andrada, Chad Denard  
Cabusbusan, Jon Wayne  
Millan, Jeff Andre

# 

# **Chapter 1** {#chapter-1}

**Introduction**

## **1.1 Background and Rationale** {#1.1-background-and-rationale}

Despite the growing presence of educational mobile applications, a significant portion of game-based learning tools continue to rely on extrinsic design principles that separate the act of playing from the act of learning. This approach places learning objectives outside the game's core loop and uses rewards such as points, badges, and unlockables to motivate engagement with content that the game itself does not make inherently interesting. Ryan and Deci (2000) demonstrate that this reliance on external reward structures consistently undermines sustained voluntary engagement, because it creates a structural contradiction between what the player wants to do and what the game is asking them to do. Prince (2004) similarly confirms that passive, unreinforced exposure to content produces retention outcomes significantly lower than those generated by active, purposeful engagement. The result is a category of educational games that are neither fully entertaining nor genuinely effective, which Söbke et al. (2020, p. 363\) famously described as "chocolate-covered broccoli."

Game-based learning offers a more productive framework when it moves beyond this extrinsic model. Byusa et al. (2022) identify incidental acquisition through motivated, repeated interaction with content as a characteristic of well-designed game-based learning environments, distinguishing it from deliberate study embedded within game reward systems. This distinction points toward a design principle in which the learning content and the gameplay mechanic are not merely adjacent but structurally identical, where the player cannot progress without engaging the content because the content is the action. Habgood and Ainsworth (2011) formalize this as the Intrinsic Integration Framework, which holds that educational games achieve maximum effectiveness when the learning material is embedded within the core mechanic such that playing and learning become a single unified experience. In their empirical study using a mathematics game, students in the intrinsically integrated condition demonstrated significantly higher learning gains and voluntarily engaged with the game up to seven times longer than those in an extrinsically designed condition, and the integration enhanced rather than diminished entertainment value.

The cognitive dimension of this design principle is equally important. Sweller (1988) established through Cognitive Load Theory that learning is most effective when the demands placed on working memory are managed and distributed across multiple encoding channels. Fernandes et al. (2018) extend this argument in a direction specifically relevant to physical interaction: drawing a to-be-learned form simultaneously activates elaborative, pictorial, and motor encoding systems, producing memory traces more durable than those generated by writing, visualization, or passive recognition of the same material. This means that when a player physically produces a character through freehand drawing as a gameplay action, the encoding that results is not incidental. It is a direct consequence of the motor engagement the game demands. The player is not studying; they are playing, and the familiarity that develops is a natural byproduct of motivated interaction.

These three convergent principles, intrinsic integration, motor encoding through drawing, and managed cognitive load, point toward a specific and largely unexplored design space: a mobile game in which freehand script character drawing is the sole core action, designed so that the player cannot play without physically producing characters, and cannot produce characters without developing familiarity with them. Baybayin, the indigenous pre-colonial writing system of the Philippines, is an exceptionally well-suited domain for demonstrating this design approach. Its syllabary of 17 base characters constitutes a closed, manageable set with low intrinsic cognitive load. Its curved, stroke-economical forms are structurally compatible with freehand touch input on mobile devices (Coulmas, 1989). And for most Filipino youth, the script already carries immediate cultural resonance through its presence in contemporary design, fashion, and public signage, providing the voluntary motivation to engage that extrinsically designed tools cannot manufacture. As Guillermo et al. (2017) document, Baybayin was in widespread everyday use among pre-colonial Filipinos across social classes, lending it a cultural authenticity that positions it not as a forgotten artifact requiring rescue, but as a living heritage worth encountering on its own terms.

The selection of Baybayin as the game's sole input domain was driven by two convergent properties. First, its closed syllabary of 17 base characters with instinctive, stroke-economical forms is structurally compatible with freehand touch input under real-time gameplay conditions, a compatibility confirmed by expert informants who noted that Baybayin characters invite natural motor production without requiring formal stroke instruction.

This study details the design and development of Salinlahi, a 2D pixel art mobile defense game in which Baybayin character drawing is the sole gameplay input. The player defends a base against waves of approaching enemies by drawing the correct Baybayin character on the touchscreen, with each character mapped to a specific enemy type, recognized in real time through on-device handwriting recognition, and processed as the game's only defensive action. The objective is not to create a game that teaches Baybayin, but to create a game that is pla	yed by using Baybayin. Salinlahi serves as a case study for the Intrinsic Integration Framework applied to script-based freehand drawing, testing whether this design approach produces the unified play-and-learn experience the framework predicts in a domain where motor encoding and cultural engagement are naturally aligned.

## **1.2 Statement of the Problem** {#1.2-statement-of-the-problem}

This study aims to address the following:

1. Existing games that incorporate cultural content follow an extrinsic design model,  separating gameplay from the learning activity, which research shows reduces voluntary engagement and long-term retention (Habgood & Ainsworth, 2011).  
2. Freehand character drawing combined with simultaneous audio and visual feedback has not been implemented as a unified live input mechanic in mobile games, leaving an untapped design space for multimodal script familiarization.  
3. No entertaining mobile game exists that puts Baybayin at the center of its core mechanic,  leaving the script culturally present but experientially invisible to Filipino youth.

## **1.3 Objectives of the Study** {#1.3-objectives-of-the-study}

### **1.3.1 General Objective** {#1.3.1-general-objective}

The general objective is to design, develop, and evaluate Salinlahi, a 2D pixel art mobile defense game that uses on-device handwriting recognition to process freehand Baybayin character drawing as the primary real-time gameplay input.

### **1.3.2 Specific Objectives** {#1.3.2-specific-objectives}

Specifically, this study aims to:

1. To design, develop, and evaluate an entertainment-focused mobile defense game where drawing characters is the core gameplay mechanic, not a separate learning activity, built around the Intrinsic Integration Framework.  
2. To determine whether combining freehand character drawing, audio pronunciation, and visual character display as a single live gameplay mechanic can build natural familiarity with the script without the player's deliberate intent to study.  
3. To assess whether an on-device Baybayin handwriting recognition system can accurately and responsively process player-drawn characters under the real-time pressure of wave-based defense gameplay.

## **1.4 Significance of the Study** {#1.4-significance-of-the-study}

This research explores the viability of heritage-driven entertainment as a vehicle for cultural engagement. By prioritizing a high-stakes gameplay loop over explicit instruction, the project demonstrates how incidental learning can bridge the gap between cultural awareness and practical familiarity. The significance of this work extends to the following stakeholders:

* **For Filipino Students and Youth.** This study provides Filipino youth aged 13 to 25 with an entertainment-focused mobile game that makes engaging with the Baybayin script a natural and rewarding experience. Rather than approaching Baybayin as an academic requirement, players interact with it voluntarily through gameplay, lowering the barrier to familiarity without the pressure of formal instruction.

* **For Game Developers and Software Engineers.** This study serves as a technical reference for implementing real-time freehand stroke recognition as a live gameplay mechanic in a mobile environment. Specifically, it demonstrates the practical application of the $P Point-Cloud Gesture Recognizer within a Unity-based mobile game, providing a documented and reproducible approach for developers exploring drawing-based input systems outside of traditional educational software.

* **For the Filipino Community.** This study is a proof of concept that a pre-colonial Filipino script, long absent from everyday life, can occupy the center of a fully functional and entertaining modern game. It was built by Filipinos, for Filipinos, as a demonstration that heritage and technology are not in conflict.

* **For Researchers in Human-Computer Interaction and Game Design.** This study contributes to an applied implementation of the Intrinsic Integration Framework (Habgood & Ainsworth, 2011\) in the context of a mobile defense game built around a non-Latin script. The findings particularly around stroke recognition accuracy, player engagement under real-time drawing pressure, and multimodal feedback response extend the existing body of work on intrinsic game design into a culturally specific and technically novel context.

## **1.5 Scope and Limitations of the Study** {#1.5-scope-and-limitations-of-the-study}

### **1.5.1 Scope** {#1.5.1-scope}

This study covers the complete design, development, and initial evaluation of Salinlahi, a 2D pixel art mobile defense game. The scope includes the design of core gameplay mechanics, enemy wave spawning and progression systems, win and loss state management, and touchscreen drawing input. The application targets Android and iOS devices and is built in portrait-mode orientation using Unity 6 LTS as the primary development engine.

The core gameplay is built around an intrinsic integration mechanic where players defend against enemy waves by drawing Baybayin characters directly on the device's touchscreen. Each enemy displays a Baybayin character on its body. The player must draw that character correctly before the enemy reaches the base. This mechanic means that playing the game and practicing Baybayin are the same single activity rather than two separate tasks layered on top of each other.

Stroke recognition is handled entirely by the $P Point-Cloud Gesture Recognizer, a template-based algorithm implemented natively in Unity. The $P algorithm requires no internet connection, no machine learning training infrastructure, and no external servers. It processes the player's drawn strokes in real time against locally stored reference templates for all 17 Baybayin consonant characters, generating a confidence score and requiring a minimum threshold of 0.60 to register a valid attack. The entire recognition pipeline runs offline and on-device.

The game includes two supporting features that reinforce the learning process. First, an audio feedback system plays a voice pronunciation clip of each Baybayin syllable whenever a character is drawn successfully. Second, a daily streak counter stored locally on the device encourages players to return each day. Both features are designed to support familiarity with the script without adding instructional screen time that would interrupt gameplay.

### **1.5.2 Limitations** {#1.5.2-limitations}

Several limitations must be acknowledged for this study. These limitations are the result of deliberate scope decisions made to keep the project feasible within the development timeline and the resources available to a student team.

First, the daily streak system is entirely offline and stored on the device's local storage. Because the system relies on the device's internal clock, players can manipulate their streak by changing the date and time settings on their phone. Connecting to an external time server to prevent this was excluded from scope because it would require persistent internet access, which conflicts with the game's fully offline design and adds infrastructure complexity that is not justified at the prototype stage.

Second, the drawing recognition accuracy of the $P algorithm is inherently subject to variation based on individual handwriting style and drawing speed. The algorithm uses geometric template matching rather than a trained neural network model, which means it provides reasonable tolerance across a wide range of stroke shapes but cannot match the accuracy of a convolutional neural network trained on a large labeled dataset. Specific known constraints include a higher false positive rate between visually similar characters such as BA and HA, reduced accuracy when strokes are drawn very quickly under time pressure, and occasional recognition failures caused by operating system gesture clipping when strokes begin near the edges of the screen.

Third, this study does not claim to produce functional Baybayin literacy, which is defined as the ability to read and compose text in the Baybayin script. The educational outcome is limited to improved character familiarity and drawing accuracy within the context of the game mechanics. Players who complete all three levels will have had repeated, pressure-tested exposure to a defined subset of the 17 Baybayin consonant characters, but this is not equivalent to a complete literacy program

Fourth, the two-application distribution model requires the team to maintain two separate build configurations from a single Unity project. While the functional difference between Salinlahi Lite and Salinlahi is managed through a build configuration flag rather than separate codebases, both builds must be independently tested, packaged, and submitted to the app stores under separate application identifiers. Any update to shared game logic, assets, or bug fixes must be verified against both builds before release. This introduces a maintenance overhead that does not exist in a single-application release and requires the team to follow a consistent build checklist to avoid configuration errors during submission.

## **1.6 Conceptual Framework** {#1.6-conceptual-framework}

The conceptual framework of Salinlahi positions the mobile game as a unified system pipeline, transforming raw player touchscreen input into both in-game combat actions and intrinsic educational outcomes. The conceptual flow focuses on real-time, offline processing within the Unity engine, ensuring seamless gameplay and immediate feedback without reliance on external servers or complex machine learning infrastructure.

*![][image1]*

### **1.6.1 Player Input and Interaction** {#1.6.1-player-input-and-interaction}

The player draws a Baybayin character freehand on the full screen. There are no input boxes or guided tracing. When an enemy appears, it displays a Baybayin character on its body. The player copies that character directly onto the screen to defeat it. The learning mechanism is not memorization but repeated motor production — drawing the same characters over and over under time pressure builds familiarity with their shapes through physical practice rather than study.

### **1.6.2 Stroke Recognition Pipeline** {#1.6.2-stroke-recognition-pipeline}

When the player lifts their finger, the captured point sequence is passed to the $P Point-Cloud Recognizer. The algorithm resamples the stroke to 32 evenly spaced points, scales them to a 250x250 unit square, and translates them to the coordinate origin to remove the effects of speed, size, and position. The processed point cloud is then compared against all 17 Baybayin character templates. If the closest match scores 0.60 or above, the recognition is accepted. If not, the stroke is rejected and the player is prompted to redraw.

### **1.6.3 Game State and Combat Resolution** {#1.6.3-game-state-and-combat-resolution}

A successful recognition triggers the matched enemy's defeat and plays a voice pronunciation clip of that Baybayin syllable at the same moment, reinforcing the connection between the character's shape and its sound. If an enemy reaches the base before a correct drawing is submitted, the base loses one heart. When all hearts are gone, the game ends. This pressure is what makes the drawing mechanic meaningful. The player draws to survive, not to complete an exercise.

### **1.6.4 Educational Output and Evaluation** {#1.6.4-educational-output-and-evaluation}

Cultural familiarity with Baybayin is a byproduct of playing rather than a separate instructional layer. Every successful attack means the player correctly read and drew the character on the enemy. Every failed recognition is immediate feedback that the stroke needs more precision. The framework is evaluated using the SUS for usability and the GEQ for engagement, because a recognition system that works but frustrates the player would defeat the framework's purpose.

## **1.7 Theoretical Framework** {#1.7-theoretical-framework}

This study is underpinned by established theories from educational psychology, game design, and computer science. These theories guide the project's design, technical development, and validation.

### **1.7.1 The Intrinsic Integration Framework** {#1.7.1-the-intrinsic-integration-framework}

The primary educational design of the game is based on the Intrinsic Integration Framework proposed by Habgood and Ainsworth (2011). This framework provides a proven solution to the most common problem in educational game design, which is the separation of gameplay and learning content. Traditional educational software often uses an extrinsic model where entertainment is applied as a superficial reward for completing academic tasks (Habgood & Ainsworth, 2011). In this old model, a player might solve a math problem or identify a letter, and then be rewarded with a fun mini game or points.

This traditional approach is known in game design literature as the chocolate covered broccoli model  according to Söbke et al. (2020, p. 363). The problem with this model is that the player's main goal is to have fun, but that goal is constantly blocked by the learning task. The learning task becomes an annoying chore that the player wants to skip or rush through just to get back to the actual game. Research shows that this extrinsic arrangement consistently fails to keep players engaged over a long period (Ryan & Deci, 2000). When the external rewards run out or become boring, the player stops engaging with the educational content entirely. This explains why survey data collected for this study showed that 43.7 percent of respondents tried to learn Baybayin using existing tools but dropped out before achieving any real confidence.

The Intrinsic Integration Framework solves this dropout problem by proving that educational games are most effective when the learning material is completely built into the core mechanics of the game (Habgood & Ainsworth, 2011). In an intrinsically integrated game, the act of playing and the act of learning become the exact same thing. There is no separation. Habgood and Ainsworth (2011) demonstrated this in their study using a mathematics game called Zombie Division. Students who played the intrinsically integrated version of the game, where mathematical problem solving was the actual attack used to defeat enemies, showed much higher learning gains compared to students who played a version where the math was separate from the combat. More importantly, the students in the intrinsic group chose to play the game voluntarily for much longer.

### **1.7.2 The Drawing Effect in Memory Retention** {#1.7.2-the-drawing-effect-in-memory-retention}

The specific input method chosen for the game is touch-based freehand drawing. The player uses their finger to draw the Baybayin characters directly onto the mobile screen. This design choice is not just for technical novelty. It is deeply rooted in cognitive psychology, specifically a well documented phenomenon known as the Drawing Effect (Fernandes et al., 2018).

Research led by Fernandes et al. (2018) proved that the physical act of drawing information creates significantly stronger and longer lasting memories compared to other study methods. In their experiments, participants were asked to memorize words and concepts using different strategies. Some participants wrote the words repeatedly, some visualized the concepts in their minds, some traced existing images, and some drew pictures of the items. The results consistently showed that drawing was far superior to all other methods for memory retention.

The specific mechanism behind this advantage was identified in Wammes et al. (2016), who conducted seven free-recall experiments to isolate the drawing effect from other possible explanations. They found that participants recalled drawn words more than twice as often as written words, and this advantage persisted even when participants had only four seconds to draw and even when the amount of information to be learned was increased. Importantly, Wammes et al. (2016) ruled out that the advantage was simply caused by deeper thinking, visual imagination, or picture-based memory effects. What made drawing unique was its ability to integrate semantic understanding, visual form, and motor action into a single, unified memory trace that is harder to forget than any of those components built in isolation. This distinction matters for Salinlahi because the Baybayin characters are visually unfamiliar. Players cannot rely on existing reading knowledge or vocabulary to remember them. Drawing is the one encoding strategy that works regardless of prior familiarity, by forcing the production of the shape rather than just its recognition.

The durability of the drawing advantage over time is also supported by research on character-level learning. Longcamp et al. (2006) trained adult participants on unfamiliar characters using either handwriting or typing and measured their recognition accuracy over several weeks. Immediately after training, both groups performed similarly. But in the weeks that followed, recognition accuracy for typed characters declined steadily, while accuracy for handwritten characters remained stable. This is a direct demonstration that handwriting does not just help in the short term but creates a fundamentally more durable memory trace. Longcamp et al. (2008) confirmed this using neuroimaging, showing that handwriting training produced lasting facilitation in character recognition by engaging motor and visual brain regions together in a way that typing did not. For Salinlahi, this means that the repeated drawing of Baybayin characters during gameplay is not just helping players recognize the characters in the moment. It is building the kind of motor-supported memory that stays with the learner even after the game session ends.

This retention benefit is consistent across different types of learners. Óttarsdóttir (2024) ran experiments with both children and adults and found that drawing produced better recall than writing in both short-term and long-term memory tests. Crucially, the advantage was largest for participants who had lower overall memory capacity for written words, meaning drawing is especially helpful precisely for learners who struggle most with traditional written study methods. Filipino youth encountering Baybayin characters for the first time are exactly this kind of learner: they have no pre-existing mental representation for these shapes, and their ability to retain them through reading or passive recognition alone is weak. Drawing provides the strongest possible encoding path for learners in this position.

### **1.7.3 The Phonological Loop and Audio-Supported Retention** {#1.7.3-the-phonological-loop-and-audio-supported-retention}

One of the core design decisions in Salinlahi is that a pronunciation audio clip plays at the exact moment a Baybayin character is successfully recognized by the system. This timing is not incidental. It is grounded in a well-established theory of how auditory input contributes to long-term memory formation, specifically the Phonological Loop model described by Baddeley et al. (1998).

Baddeley et al. (1998) proposed that the phonological loop is a dedicated component of working memory whose primary function is to temporarily store unfamiliar sound sequences while a more permanent memory record is being built. Their review of word-learning experiments across normal adults, children with language disorders, and neuropsychological patients consistently showed that the phonological loop plays a direct and measurable role in converting short-term auditory exposure into stable long-term memory, especially for sounds that have no existing equivalent in the learner's prior knowledge. In practical terms, this means that when a learner hears a new sound while simultaneously engaging in a meaningful action, that sound is more likely to be retained over time than if it were heard passively or in isolation.

This principle applies directly to Baybayin character learning in Salinlahi. The syllabic sounds of the Baybayin characters, such as "ba," "ka," "ga," and "ha," are not foreign to Filipino players. These sounds already exist in their spoken vocabulary. What is new is the association between those familiar sounds and the unfamiliar visual and motor form of each character. When the game plays a pronunciation clip at the exact moment the player completes a successful drawing, it is activating an already-familiar phonological representation and binding it to a just-completed visual and motor experience. Baddeley et al. (1998) describe this kind of binding as the most efficient path into long-term retention because the learner does not need to build the sound representation from scratch. They only need to anchor it to the new character form. The immediate timing of the audio clip is what makes this anchoring possible.

Mayer's (2009) Cognitive Theory of Multimedia Learning provides a complementary explanation from a different angle. Mayer's framework argues that meaningful learning happens when verbal information (processed through the auditory channel) and pictorial information (processed through the visual channel) are actively integrated in working memory rather than processed independently. When both channels are engaged at the same time and around the same meaningful event, the resulting memory representation is richer and more retrievable than what either channel produces on its own. In Salinlahi, the moment of successful recognition activates all three channels simultaneously: the player has just finished drawing the character (motor and visual), sees the enemy defeated (visual consequence), and hears the syllable pronounced (auditory). These are not separate events. They are one event, which is exactly the condition Mayer's framework identifies as most favorable for durable encoding.

Moreno and Mayer (2007) extended this argument into interactive environments specifically. In their studies of game-like and simulation-based learning tools, they found that multimodal feedback, meaning feedback that delivers information through more than one sensory channel at the same time, produced significantly stronger retention and comprehension gains compared to unimodal feedback or feedback that was delayed after the learning action. Crucially, this advantage was greatest when the learner was actively constructing something rather than passively receiving instruction. The act of construction gave the feedback an anchor point in the learner's ongoing experience, which made the information easier to store and easier to retrieve later. This finding directly supports Salinlahi's feedback timing. The audio clip arrives at the moment the player finishes constructing the character, which is exactly when the encoding anchor is strongest and the phonological input can do the most work in consolidating a durable memory trace.

Together, these three theoretical positions explain why audio feedback in Salinlahi is not just a feature for polish or atmosphere. It is a functional component of the game's retention mechanism. The phonological loop model explains how immediate auditory input is converted into long-term memory during active tasks. The Cognitive Theory of Multimedia Learning explains why multi-channel simultaneous encoding produces more durable memory than single-channel encoding. And Moreno and Mayer's interactive multimodal feedback research explains why the moment of successful drawing is the optimal moment for audio delivery. Salinlahi is designed so that these three effects operate together every time a player defeats an enemy.

### **1.7.4 The Point Cloud Gesture Recognition Model** {#1.7.4-the-point-cloud-gesture-recognition-model}

To make the freehand drawing mechanic work in a mobile game, the system needs a way to instantly and accurately check if the player's drawing matches the correct Baybayin character. To solve this, the theoretical framework relies on a highly efficient mathematical solution known as the $P Point-Cloud Recognizer algorithm.

The $P algorithm is a two dimensional gesture recognizer specifically designed for the rapid prototyping of user interfaces. It is part of a famous family of algorithms known in computer science as the dollar family. Previous versions of the algorithm, like $1 and $N, had significant limitations. They treated drawings as an ordered sequence of points. This meant that if a player drew a circle clockwise, and the template was saved as a counter clockwise circle, the algorithm would fail to recognize it completely.

The breakthrough of the $P algorithm is that it completely ignores stroke number, stroke order, and stroke direction. Instead of viewing the drawing as a strict timeline of strokes, it views the drawing as an unordered cloud of points scattered on a two dimensional plane. As long as the final visual shape looks correct, the algorithm will recognize it.

### **1.7.5 Entertainment Driven Incidental Learning Theory** {#1.7.5-entertainment-driven-incidental-learning-theory}

The final theoretical pillar of the project is Incidental Learning Theory, specifically focusing on entertainment driven cultural acquisition (Leon et al., 2025). Incidental learning is defined as the acquisition of knowledge as a natural byproduct of engaging in an activity that is primarily meant for entertainment. In this scenario, the user does not have a conscious intention to study or learn. Their only motivation is to enjoy the activity, but they absorb complex information anyway.

The most prominent modern examples of incidental learning are seen in the global consumption of entertainment media, specifically Korean dramas and Japanese anime. Research studies focused on the Hallyu wave, or the Korean cultural wave, show that fans who regularly watch Korean entertainment passively acquire Korean vocabulary, understand sentence structures, and recognize cultural customs without ever taking a formal language class (Leon et al., 2025). The viewers do not watch the shows to study. They watch for the story and the emotional engagement. The learning happens automatically because the cultural content is deeply woven into the entertainment.

By leveraging Incidental Learning Theory, the development team can confidently build a game that focuses purely on making the mechanics fun, knowing that the educational outcomes will naturally follow as a byproduct of that fun.

## **1.8 Technical and Operational Definitions** {#1.8-technical-and-operational-definitions}

* **Active Recall:** The core mechanic where players must immediately remember and draw the Baybayin character seen on an enemy to trigger an attack and defend the base.

* **Baybayin:** The ancient Brahmic script of the pre-colonial Philippines; operationally used as the primary "input ammunition" required to progress through levels.

* **Castle/Base Defense:** The specific game sub-genre where the player protects a stationary point (the Shrine) from waves of approaching enemies.

* **Chain Movement:** The movement logic where multiple enemy characters (forming a word) maintain a fixed distance while traveling toward the player's base.

* **Disguised Learning:** The design philosophy where educational content is embedded in the game mechanics so that players master the 17 characters without formal quizzes.

* **Kadiliman:** The antagonist force in the game representing cultural erasure; manifested as shadow entities that must be defeated by the light of the script.

* **Kudlit:** A diacritical mark used to modify vowel sounds; operationally used as an advanced gameplay modifier that increases difficulty in later waves.

* **Stroke Validation:** The mathematical process/algorithm that checks the player's hand-drawn input against a reference template to determine if an attack is successful.

* **Wave:** A group of enemies that attack the player's base in a specific sequence, with difficulty increasing as the game progresses.

# **Chapter 2** {#chapter-2}

**Review of Related Literature**

This chapter reviews the literature and existing applications relevant to the design and development of Salinlahi, a 2D pixel art mobile defense game where freehand Baybayin character drawing serves as the sole gameplay input. Because the study's central argument is about game design rather than script instruction, the review is organized around the design and cognitive principles that make the mechanic work: the evidence base for game-based learning and intrinsic integration; the cognitive mechanisms through which physical drawing produces memory advantages; the multimodal encoding conditions that maximize the learning effect of combined drawing, audio, and visual feedback; the technical constraints governing real-time on-device gesture recognition; and the precedent cases for entertainment-driven incidental learning. The chapter concludes with an analysis of six related applications and a synthesis that maps the collective findings onto Salinlahi's design rationale.

## **2.1. Related Studies** {#2.1.-related-studies}

The problem Salinlahi addresses is not a lack of awareness of Baybayin among Filipino youth. Pre-development survey data collected for this study confirmed that 96.8% of respondents were aware of the script, and 88.9% rated its cultural significance at 4 or 5 out of 5\. The problem is the failure of existing tools to convert that awareness into practical familiarity: 43.7% of respondents had actively attempted to learn Baybayin through available resources and abandoned the effort before achieving confidence. The literature on passive learning environments identifies the structural reason for this dropout pattern. Oglu and Babazade (2024) find that passive, recognition-based tools consistently fail to sustain engagement in subjects that require active cognitive processing, because they place the burden of motivation entirely on the learner's pre-existing intent. Brinton et al. (2008) similarly argue that heritage script and language acquisition is most effective when learners are given repeated opportunities for active, purposeful use of the script in contexts that carry personal meaning, a condition that flashcard and quiz-based tools structurally cannot satisfy. The five sections that follow review the evidence for a design approach that replaces passive recognition with active, mechanic-driven production.

### **2.1.1 Game-Based Learning as a Pedagogical Approach** {#2.1.1-game-based-learning-as-a-pedagogical-approach}

Game-based learning (GBL) has been extensively studied as a solution to the engagement and retention problems associated with passive instructional approaches. A systematic review by Byusa et al. (2022) concluded that GBL significantly improves student motivation and understanding of complex conceptual content by embedding learning objectives within an interactive and emotionally engaging framework. The motivational architecture of games, particularly the use of challenge, feedback, and progression systems creates conditions for deeper cognitive processing than those typically found in passive instructional environments.The value of GBL is not uniformly realized, however. Tene et al. (2025), reviewing serious games across STEM disciplines, confirm that well-designed games facilitate both conceptual learning and applied skill development, while poorly designed ones reproduce the passivity they purport to replace. Kalogiannakis et al. (2021) similarly find that games produce sustained engagement only when their mechanics are meaningfully aligned with the subject matter, rather than simply rewarding players for completing academic tasks. This conditionality is central to Salinlahi's design: the study does not treat GBL as inherently effective, but applies it under the specific conditions the literature identifies as necessary a direct, structural connection between the act of playing and the act of learning.

### **2.1.2 Intrinsic Integration Versus the Chocolate-Covered Broccoli Fallacy** {#2.1.2-intrinsic-integration-versus-the-chocolate-covered-broccoli-fallacy}

The most critical distinction in the GBL literature is the one between extrinsic and intrinsic models of educational game design. Söbke et al. (2020, p. 363\) identified what has become known as the chocolate-covered broccoli flaw, in which developers treat entertainment as a coating applied to an educational core rather than as an emergent property of a well-designed learning experience. In this model, players are rewarded with game elements such as points, badges, or cutscenes for completing educational tasks, but the tasks themselves remain structurally separate from the gameplay. Ryan and Deci (2000) identify this contradiction as the defining weakness of extrinsic motivation, arguing that external rewards consistently undermine sustained engagement and reduce intrinsic interest in the underlying activity.The cognitive foundation for why this structural separation fails is provided by Cognitive Load Theory. Sweller (1988) established that learning is most effective when the demands placed on working memory are managed and distributed efficiently across available encoding channels. When an educational game separates the learning task from the play task, it compounds cognitive load rather than managing it: the player must simultaneously attend to the game's entertainment demands and the learning module's instructional demands as two competing cognitive processes. Intrinsic integration resolves this by collapsing the two into a single unified action, so that the cognitive resources directed toward succeeding in the game are the same resources being used to process the learning content.The theoretical solution to the chocolate-covered broccoli flaw is the Intrinsic Integration Framework proposed by Habgood and Ainsworth (2011). This framework holds that educational games achieve their maximum effectiveness when the learning content is embedded in the core gameplay mechanic, such that the act of playing and the act of learning become a single unified action. In their empirical study using a mathematics game called Zombie Division, Habgood and Ainsworth demonstrated that students who played the intrinsically integrated version where mathematical problem-solving was the attack mechanic used to defeat enemies showed significantly higher learning gains than those in an extrinsically designed control condition. Critically, students in the intrinsic group also spent up to seven times longer engaging with the game voluntarily, indicating that the integration of learning into core mechanics does not come at the cost of entertainment value but in fact enhances it.In Salinlahi, this framework is applied directly. The player draws Baybayin characters on the screen to attack enemies. There is no separate learning module, no quiz that interrupts play, and no badge awarded for recognizing a character. The only reward for correctly drawing a character is the destruction of an enemy and continued survival. Baybayin's closed syllabary of 17 base characters constitutes a manageable cognitive set, its cultural resonance among Filipino youth provides the voluntary motivation that extrinsically designed tools cannot manufacture, and the absence of any visual overlap with the Latin alphabet ensures that character familiarity must be genuinely built rather than inferred from prior knowledge.

### **2.1.3 Drawing as a Learning Mechanic: The Cognitive Basis** {#2.1.3-drawing-as-a-learning-mechanic:-the-cognitive-basis}

The specific mechanic through which Salinlahi operationalizes character learning is touch-based freehand drawing on a mobile screen. This design choice is supported by a well-established body of cognitive research on drawing as a memory and learning strategy. Fernandes et al. (2018), in a widely cited review of empirical drawing research, demonstrated that drawing to-be-learned information is a reliable and consistently replicable means of improving memory performance. Their studies found that memory gains from drawing were greater than those produced by other encoding strategies, including writing, mental visualization, viewing images, and even tracing the target information. The authors propose that drawing improves memory by simultaneously engaging elaborative, pictorial, and motor codes, creating a richer and more integrated cognitive representation than any single-modality encoding strategy can produce. This multimodal advantage is consistent with Sweller's (1988) Cognitive Load Theory: distributing encoding across elaborative, visual, and motor channels reduces the load on any single channel while increasing the robustness and retrievability of the resulting memory trace.

The foundational evidence for drawing as a retention tool comes from Wammes et al. (2016), who conducted seven free-recall experiments directly comparing drawing against writing and other encoding strategies. Their results showed that participants recalled drawn words more than twice as often as written words, and this advantage held even when encoding time was reduced to as little as four seconds per item and when list length was increased. The quality of the drawings did not matter, meaning the memory benefit was available to all learners regardless of drawing skill. Crucially, Wammes et al. (2016) ruled out elaboration, visual imagery, and picture superiority as sufficient explanations, concluding that drawing benefits memory specifically because it promotes the seamless integration of semantic, visual, and motor information into a single, durable memory trace. This finding is directly relevant to Baybayin character learning: because the characters are visually unfamiliar to most Filipino youth and carry no prior semantic association, the motor act of producing each character through drawing creates the richest possible encoding path available, one that cannot be replicated by viewing or selecting characters alone.

The durability of this retention advantage, not only its immediate strength, is supported by research on motor memory in character learning. Longcamp et al. (2006) trained adults on newly learned characters using either handwriting or typing methods over three weeks. Immediately after training, both groups showed similar recognition rates. However, over the following weeks, recognition accuracy for typed characters gradually declined, while accuracy for handwritten characters remained stable. This pattern demonstrates that the motor information encoded during handwriting functions as a long-term memory support that typing does not provide. Longcamp et al. (2008) extended this finding using neuroimaging, showing that handwriting training produced stronger and longer-lasting facilitation in recognizing character orientation, and that this advantage was associated with greater activity in brain regions involved in action execution and motor imagery. Taken together, these studies make a clear case that the physical production of a character creates a qualitatively different and more durable memory representation than any recognition-only task can produce.

This applies directly to Salinlahi's design in a practical sense. When a player draws a Baybayin character in Salinlahi, they are not pressing a button or selecting an answer, they are physically producing the character. This engages all three encoding components simultaneously: elaboration, through recognition and decision-making about the correct character; pictorial processing, through visual attention to the form being produced; and motor processing, through the physical act of tracing the strokes on the touchscreen. The effect is durable enough to persist even in clinical populations: Matorina et al. (2024) documented drawing-enhanced retention in a patient with severe anterograde amnesia, suggesting that the motor encoding component operates through memory pathways partially independent of declarative memory systems. This cognitive advantage is further compounded by a structural feature of Baybayin itself. Unlike the Roman alphabet, in which written characters correspond to individual phonemes, Baybayin is an abugida in which each base character represents a complete consonant-vowel syllabic unit. Expert consultation conducted for this study confirmed that the most persistent error among new Baybayin learners is what can be termed the spelling fallacy: the tendency to decompose syllables into their constituent Roman letters and search for a separate Baybayin character for each. A learner attempting to write "Nicky," for instance, will instinctively look for characters representing N, I, C, K, and Y individually, when Baybayin requires only two characters representing the syllabic units NI and KI. Passive recognition tools, multiple choice interfaces, flashcard apps, and character-matching exercises, cannot correct this error because they present characters in isolation without requiring the learner to produce them in syllabic context. Freehand drawing of a complete character as a single gestural unit directly counteracts the spelling fallacy by treating each character as a unified motor program. The player who draws the BA character in Salinlahi is not assembling B and A; they are producing a single shape whose identity is the syllable. This is precisely the cognitive reframing that expert informants identify as the threshold between surface familiarity and functional script comprehension. Further support for the retention depth of drawing comes from Óttarsdóttir (2024), who conducted two-way crossover experiments with 134 children and 262 adults, finding that drawing produced superior recall over writing in both short-term and longer-term memory conditions, and that this advantage was especially pronounced among participants who had difficulty with written word recall, a subgroup that includes learners encountering an unfamiliar script for the first time.

Hsiao and Chen (2016) reinforced this principle in a direct educational game context, demonstrating that a gesture-interactive game-based learning approach significantly improved both cognitive learning performance and motor skill development compared to traditional activity-based approaches. The physical act of performing gestures to interact with learning content creates an embodied learning effect, in which the motor experience of producing a shape reinforces its cognitive representation. Tadiboyina et al. (2024), evaluating a gesture-interactive alphabet learning game, confirmed that gesture-based interfaces are well-suited for character and alphabet training precisely because they engage fine motor, visual, and cognitive systems simultaneously.

The drawing mechanic, however, is only educationally effective if the system can reliably validate what the player draws under real-time gameplay constraints. A recognition system that is too strict will frustrate the player and interrupt the game loop; one that is too permissive will not require genuine character production and will undermine the motor encoding mechanism. This tension between recognition reliability and gameplay fluidity motivates the technical design choices reviewed in Section 2.1.5.

### **2.1.4 Multimodal Encoding in Interactive Learning Environments** {#2.1.4-multimodal-encoding-in-interactive-learning-environments}

The cognitive case for combining freehand drawing with simultaneous audio feedback and visual recognition as a unified gameplay mechanic is grounded in Mayer's (2009) Cognitive Theory of Multimedia Learning, which holds that meaningful learning is achieved when verbal and pictorial information are processed through separate cognitive channels and actively integrated into a single coherent mental representation. Where Fernandes et al. (2018) establish the memory advantages of drawing as a motor encoding strategy, Mayer's framework supplies the complementary mechanism: audio feedback delivered at the moment of a successful drawing activates the phonological channel simultaneously with the visual and motoric channels already engaged by the act of production itself, producing a richer and more durable multimodal trace than any single-channel encoding strategy can achieve in isolation. Moreno and Mayer (2007) extend this principle to interactive contexts, demonstrating that multimodal feedback in learning environments produces significantly greater retention and understanding than unimodal or delayed feedback conditions, particularly when learners are actively constructing rather than passively receiving information.

The role of audio specifically in building durable memory for unfamiliar phonological forms is explained by Baddeley et al. (1998), who proposed that the phonological loop, a component of working memory, evolved precisely to store unfamiliar sound patterns temporarily while more permanent long-term memory records are being constructed. Their review of word-learning studies across normal adults, children, and neuropsychological patients shows that the phonological loop plays a direct and critical role in converting short-term auditory input into stable long-term memory representations, especially for sounds that have no prior equivalent in the learner's existing knowledge. This is particularly relevant to Baybayin, because the syllabic values of the script's characters, such as "ba," "ka," and "ga," are already part of Filipino speech and therefore carry existing phonological representations in the learner's long-term memory. When a pronunciation clip plays at the exact moment a character is successfully drawn, the auditory input does not need to build a new phonological form from scratch. Instead, it links an already-familiar sound to a newly-produced visual-motor experience, giving the learner a fast and stable route into long-term retention of that character's identity. Baddeley et al. (1998) describe this process as a phonological loop function: temporary auditory storage supports the construction of permanent memory, and repeated auditory exposure during active production accelerates that construction.

This theoretical convergence finds further support in Paivio's (1991) Dual Coding Theory, which predicts that encoding simultaneously engaging both the imagistic and verbal representational systems produces retrieval advantages that neither system alone can replicate. When the third channel of motoric encoding identified by Fernandes et al. (2018) is added under the time pressure of a live defensive gameplay loop, the conditions for maximum multimodal retention convergence are fully satisfied. The neuroimaging evidence from Longcamp et al. (2008) provides a biological basis for this convergence: handwriting training was found to activate brain regions associated with both visual recognition and motor imagery simultaneously, and characters learned through handwriting showed stronger and more stable recognition weeks after training compared to characters learned through typing alone. This means that the combination of motoric, visual, and phonological encoding active during Salinlahi's gameplay loop is not just theoretically predicted to produce durable retention but is also neurologically consistent with how motor-supported character learning works in the brain.

Despite this well-established theoretical foundation, the practical application of this convergence to live mobile gameplay remains largely unrealized by existing tools. The applications reviewed in this study address individual components of the model in isolation: Aralin Baybayin engages motor production through freehand drawing but presents it within an explicitly instructional context stripped of gameplay pressure; Learn Baybayin provides audio pronunciation support and visual character display but requires no active character production from the learner; and Magic Touch (Nitrome, 2015\) combines freehand drawing with real-time recognition under time pressure but applies these to invented symbols with no phonetic identity and therefore no audio feedback capable of reinforcing a meaningful cultural connection. No reviewed application combines all three modalities, freehand character production, immediate audio pronunciation feedback, and on-device real-time visual recognition, within a single uninterrupted gameplay action. This gap is not merely a design oversight; it represents a structural failure to operationalize the multimodal encoding conditions the literature identifies as most conducive to durable character familiarity. Salinlahi addresses this gap directly: the player draws a Baybayin character under wave-based time pressure, receives a pronunciation audio clip at the precise moment the character is successfully recognized, and observes the immediate visual consequence of that recognition in the form of enemy defeat. These are not sequential instructional steps delivered across separate screens. They are simultaneous outputs of a single gameplay action, delivered at the moment when motor, auditory, and visual encoding channels are jointly engaged and a meaningful consequence makes the encoding effectively significant.

### **2.1.5 Gesture Recognition for Mobile Drawing Input** {#2.1.5-gesture-recognition-for-mobile-drawing-input}

A mobile game in which freehand character drawing is the sole gameplay input requires a recognition system that can evaluate drawn input accurately, offline, and within milliseconds. This constraint eliminates cloud-based machine learning inference as a practical option, since network latency would interrupt gameplay continuity. The relevant literature on mobile gesture recognition identifies two broad technical approaches: trained machine learning classifiers and template-based recognizers. Each carries distinct tradeoffs for a project with Salinlahi's constraints.Trained machine learning approaches, particularly convolutional neural network (CNN) classifiers, have demonstrated high recognition accuracy for handwritten character inputs. CNN-based recognition, however, requires a labeled training dataset, offline training infrastructure, and a model binary that can be prohibitively large for on-device mobile deployment. For a development team without access to a curated Baybayin handwriting dataset, this approach introduces prerequisites that fall outside the study's scope. Furthermore, the accuracy reported by CNN-based systems is typically measured against clean, deliberate handwriting samples; performance under gameplay conditions, where strokes are drawn quickly under time pressure, has not been independently characterized.Template-based gesture recognizers offer a complementary approach: rather than learning from a training corpus, they compare an incoming gesture against a set of predefined reference templates using geometric distance metrics. The most influential template-based recognizers for touch input belong to the dollar family (Wobbrock et al., 2007). The foundational $1 recognizer demonstrated that a simple Euclidean distance metric applied to normalized, resampled point sequences could achieve high recognition accuracy with minimal templates. Its successor $N extended support to multi-stroke gestures but preserved an assumption of consistent stroke order, meaning two drawings of the same character produced in different stroke sequences would be treated as distinct gestures (Anthony & Wobbrock, 2010).The $P Point-Cloud Recognizer (Vatavu et al., 2012\) resolved the stroke order limitation by discarding the drawing timeline entirely. Rather than treating a gesture as an ordered sequence of strokes, $P treats it as an unordered cloud of points in two-dimensional space. Recognition is performed by finding the assignment of drawn points to template points that minimizes total pairwise Euclidean distance, using an approximation of the Hungarian algorithm. This makes $P invariant to stroke order, stroke count, and stroke direction: as long as the final visual shape matches a template, the algorithm will recognize it regardless of how the player drew it. For a game designed to introduce Filipino youth to Baybayin without prior instruction in the script's historical stroke conventions, this invariance is a design requirement rather than merely a convenience. Requiring historically accurate stroke order would impose a constraint orthogonal to the game's educational objective, which is character familiarity and production accuracy rather than scribal technique.The $P algorithm's processing pipeline is computationally lightweight by design. It resamples an incoming stroke to a fixed number of evenly-spaced points, scales the point cloud to a normalized reference square, translates it to the origin, and computes the minimum-cost matching against stored templates, the entire pipeline operates in sub-millisecond time on modern mobile hardware. The tradeoff relative to CNN-based approaches is a lower theoretical accuracy ceiling and reduced robustness to highly variable drawing styles. However, for a closed character set of 17 Baybayin base characters with a controllable template collection, the accuracy ceiling is sufficient for the game's requirements. The recognition system is not evaluating handwriting quality against a universal standard; it is evaluating whether a drawn shape is consistent with a specific character template within a defined confidence threshold. This positions $P as the architecturally correct recognizer for Salinlahi's context: offline, real-time, stroke-order-agnostic, and deployable without a training dataset.

### **2.1.6 Entertainment-Driven Incidental Cultural and Language Learning** {#2.1.6-entertainment-driven-incidental-cultural-and-language-learning}

One of the most well-documented real-world examples of intrinsically motivated learning through entertainment is the phenomenon of incidental language and cultural acquisition through popular media. Incidental learning is defined as the acquisition of knowledge as a byproduct of engaging with an activity whose primary purpose is not instruction, where the learner does not consciously intend to learn and is primarily motivated by enjoyment rather than educational goals (Leon et al., 2025).

The Korean Wave, or Hallyu, has generated a substantial body of research on how entertainment-driven media exposure produces genuine cultural and linguistic acquisition without formal instruction. Leon et al. (2025), in a qualitative case study with Filipino Hallyu fans, found that repeated exposure to Korean dramas, K-pop, and variety shows facilitated passive acquisition of Korean vocabulary, sentence structures, and pronunciation patterns among participants who had never received formal Korean language instruction. This is precisely the mechanism Salinlahi applies to Baybayin: the player does not open the game to study the script, but the desire to survive the next wave of enemies produces the same kind of repeated, motivated engagement with the cultural content. The survey data collected for this study confirms this dynamic directly: 75.4% of respondents reported knowing a foreign script better than Baybayin because it was tied to entertainment they already voluntarily consumed.

The parallel holds in the context of anime and Japanese language acquisition. Shintaku (2022) found that anime served not only as a motivational driver for formal Japanese study but as a site of active literacy practice. Bacani and Hatami (2024), in a study of native English-speaking anime viewers, confirmed that incidental vocabulary acquisition occurs through anime viewing, with post-test scores demonstrating measurable gains after viewing subtitled episodes. The critical difference between these media precedents and Salinlahi is one of modality and degree of integration: in Salinlahi, the cultural content of the Baybayin script is not ambient background. It is the mechanic. The player cannot act without engaging with it, making the acquisition not merely incidental but structurally guaranteed by the game loop itself.

## **2.2 Related Applications** {#2.2-related-applications}

This section analyzes six applications and media relevant to Salinlahi's design. The first two are existing Baybayin learning tools that represent the current state of the landscape the study positions itself against. The next two are games whose core drawing input mechanics most directly parallel Salinlahi's design. The final two are entertainment media included as precedent cases for the entertainment-driven incidental learning mechanism reviewed in Section 2.1.6; their inclusion is justified by their role as empirical evidence for that mechanism rather than as software comparisons.

### **2.2.1 Learn Baybayin (Team Gavin)** {#2.2.1-learn-baybayin-(team-gavin)}

Learn Baybayin is a free Android application developed by Team Gavin that provides users with an instructional platform for learning Baybayin characters. The app employs spaced-repetition techniques to aid memorization, offers bidirectional translation between Baybayin and the Latin alphabet, and includes an audio pronunciation component for each character. Interactive quizzes serve as the primary assessment mechanism, providing users with immediate feedback on their recognition of individual characters.

**Table 1\.**  
*Learn Baybayin: Strengths and Weaknesses*

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
| ----- | ----- |
| Provides a structured, accessible introduction to all 17 Baybayin characters in a single mobile platform. | The core interaction is quiz-based recognition rather than active production, meaning the player identifies characters rather than drawing them, which limits motor memory development. |
| The spaced-repetition algorithm supports retention by scheduling character review at optimal intervals. | The instructional model is fundamentally passive and extrinsically motivated, relying on score feedback rather than embedding character learning into a meaningful activity. |
| Audio pronunciation support creates a phonetic connection between the character's visual form and its spoken equivalent. | There is no narrative, emotional stakes, or game world to provide context for the learning, making sustained engagement dependent entirely on the user's pre-existing motivation to study Baybayin. |

### **2.2.2 Aralin Baybayin (Jadaone et al., 2023\)** {#2.2.2-aralin-baybayin-(jadaone-et-al.,-2023)}

Aralin Baybayin is an Intelligent Tutoring System (ITS) developed by researchers at the Polytechnic University of the Philippines and presented at the 11th International Conference on Computer and Communications Management in 2023\. The system uses a convolutional neural network model to recognize handwritten Baybayin characters submitted by users, achieving a training accuracy of 99.16% and a testing accuracy of 98.60%. The application provides progressive lessons, character recognition exercises, and transliteration support, and was evaluated by IT experts and third-year BSIT students using the ISO 25010 software quality standard (Jadaone et al., 2023).

**Table 2\.**  
*Aralin Baybayin: Strengths and Weaknesses*

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
| ----- | ----- |
| The use of CNN-based handwritten character recognition means the system accepts freehand user input, engaging the learner's motor system in a way that passive flash-card tools do not. | The system is designed as a tutoring tool rather than a game, meaning the learning activity remains explicitly instructional. The learner is always aware they are in a lesson, which limits the intrinsic motivation benefits associated with play. |
| The ISO 25010 evaluation framework ensures a rigorous approach to software quality and user experience assessment. | The ITS format structures interaction as teacher-student, which research identifies as a less effective motivational model than games that embed learning into challenge-based play (Habgood and Ainsworth, 2011). |
| Progressive lesson structure ensures that learners are not overwhelmed by all 17 characters simultaneously. | Primarily designed for academic or institutional contexts, lacking the entertainment-first orientation that would appeal to casual users outside of formal study settings. |

### **2.2.3 Okami (Clover Studios, 2006\)** {#2.2.3-okami-(clover-studios,-2006)}

Okami is a critically acclaimed action-adventure game developed by Clover Studios and published by Capcom for the PlayStation 2 in 2006\. The game's primary and defining mechanic is the Celestial Brush, a system in which the player pauses the game at any moment and draws shapes directly onto the screen. Each shape functions as a distinct command: drawing a circle summons the sun, drawing a line through an enemy executes a slash attack, and drawing a loop summons a wind gust. All 13 brush techniques are learned progressively as the player advances through the story (Clover Studios, 2006).

**Table 3\.**  
*Okami: Strengths and Weaknesses*

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
| ----- | ----- |
| Establishes a compelling proof of concept that drawing directly on the screen can function as a primary, satisfying game mechanic rather than a novelty feature. | The drawn shapes in Okami are abstract visual gestures with no real-world referent. They do not correspond to any existing writing system or symbol set, meaning the player acquires no transferable knowledge from mastering the mechanic. |
| The progressive introduction of brush techniques mirrors Salinlahi's design principle of scaffolding character introduction across gameplay stages. | The drawing mechanic in Okami is used primarily for combat and puzzle solutions rather than as a continuous, high-frequency interaction. In Salinlahi, drawing is the only available action, creating a far higher repetition density for each character. |
| The integration of drawing into core combat and exploration mechanics demonstrates that gesture input can be both mechanically deep and narratively meaningful simultaneously. | The game is a console and PC title, making its drawing mechanic dependent on specific controller inputs. Salinlahi's touchscreen-native design eliminates this hardware dependency. |

### **2.2.4 Magic Touch: Wizard for Hire (Nitrome, 2015\)** {#2.2.4-magic-touch:-wizard-for-hire-(nitrome,-2015)}

Magic Touch: Wizard for Hire is a free mobile defense game developed by Nitrome and released on iOS, Android, and the Amazon App Store in 2015, reaching over six million downloads within its first few months of release. The game's core mechanic is structurally near-identical to Salinlahi's: enemies descend from the top of the screen toward the player's base, each one carrying a balloon marked with a symbol. The player must draw that symbol on the touchscreen to pop the balloon and eliminate the enemy before it reaches the ground.

The game is relevant to this study as the most direct structural precedent for Salinlahi's core defense-and-drawing loop, and as commercial validation that a game whose sole mechanic is drawing symbols on a touchscreen under pressure can achieve mass-market appeal and sustained engagement (Nitrome, 2015).

**Table 4\.**  
*Magic Touch: Wizard for Hire: Strengths and Weaknesses*

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
| ----- | ----- |
| Provides direct commercial proof that a mobile defense game built entirely around drawing symbols on a touchscreen is engaging, accessible, and capable of reaching mass audiences, with over six million downloads. | The symbols used in Magic Touch are abstract, invented shapes with no real-world referent or cultural meaning. Drawing them produces no transferable knowledge outside the game itself. |
| The combo mechanic demonstrates that symbol-matching under pressure can create genuine strategic depth rather than mere reaction gameplay. | Because the symbols are arbitrary and fictitious, the repetition that builds player skill within the game does not correspond to the acquisition of any real script, language, or cultural content. |
| The game's progressive difficulty and enemy variety demonstrate that a single-mechanic design built around drawing can sustain player interest across extended play sessions. | The game uses symbols that are visually simple and geometrically distinct from one another by design, making recognition trivially easy at a glance. Baybayin characters require genuine learning to distinguish, meaning Salinlahi's design must account for a higher initial cognitive load. |

### **2.2.5 Golden Kamuy (Geno Studio, 2018\)** {#2.2.5-golden-kamuy-(geno-studio,-2018)}

Golden Kamuy is a Japanese anime television series animated by Geno Studio, based on Satoru Noda's manga. The series is set in the Meiji era of early twentieth century Hokkaido, Japan, and integrates extensive depictions of Ainu indigenous culture into every episode, including the Ainu language, spiritual practices, hunting and survival techniques, and social customs. Following the manga's conclusion in 2022, the Mainichi Newspaper credited the series with spreading Ainu culture throughout the world, and youth visits to Ainu cultural sites in Hokkaido increased in the period following the series' peak popularity (Ito, 2022). However, Ito's (2022) peer-reviewed analysis also identifies significant limitations, arguing that despite the creator's care, the show cannot escape reproducing race-related discourses that reflect Japan's colonial legacy.

**Table 5\.**  
*Golden Kamuy: Strengths and Weaknesses*

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
| ----- | ----- |
| Documents at scale show that entertainment-first media centered on a historically marginalized indigenous culture can produce widespread cultural awareness and increased public interest as a byproduct of audience engagement, without requiring any instructional framing. | Learning in Golden Kamuy is passive and ambient. The viewer absorbs Ainu culture by watching characters perform it, but is never required to actively recall, reproduce, or apply any cultural knowledge to continue experiencing the content. |
| The documented increase in public interest in Ainu culture following the series' popularity demonstrates that entertainment-driven cultural exposure can produce real-world behavioral outcomes beyond the viewing experience itself. | Ito (2022) argues that despite the creator's evident care, the show's Ainu language allocation patterns reproduce colonial-era race-related discourses, positioning Ainu culture as content for a non-Ainu majority audience rather than centering it on its own terms. |
| The creator's extensive use of academic and linguistic consultation demonstrates that entertainment and cultural fidelity are not mutually exclusive, supporting Salinlahi's design goal of making Baybayin both engaging and accurately represented. | As a linear narrative medium, Golden Kamuy offers no player agency or active participation. Viewers observe Ainu culture practiced by characters but do not practice it themselves, which limits the depth of acquisition compared to a game-based interaction model. |

### **2.2.6 Mr. Sunshine (Studio Dragon, 2018\)** {#2.2.6-mr.-sunshine-(studio-dragon,-2018)}

Mr. Sunshine is a South Korean historical drama series produced by Studio Dragon and broadcast on tvN in 2018\. The series is set during the late Joseon Dynasty and the period of Japanese imperial encroachment in Korea, depicting the lives of Korean aristocrats, independence fighters, and foreign military officers against the backdrop of the country's resistance to colonization. The drama became one of the highest-rated cable dramas in South Korean television history at the time of its broadcast and attracted a large international audience through streaming platforms.

Mr. Sunshine is relevant to this study as an example of the phenomenon documented by Adway (2023), in which historical television drama functions as a vehicle for history learning through narrative engagement. The drama produced a documented surge in public interest in the late Joseon period and the Korean independence movement among younger Korean and international audiences. The parallel to Salinlahi is one of mechanisms: just as Mr. Sunshine's viewers absorbed historical knowledge because the story required them to understand its context to follow the plot, Salinlahi's players will acquire Baybayin literacy because the game requires them to draw Baybayin characters to survive.

**Table 6\.**  
*Mr. Sunshine: Strengths and Weaknesses*

| Strengths | Weaknesses |
| ----- | ----- |
| Demonstrates at scale that historical and cultural content embedded within emotionally compelling narrative entertainment produces genuine knowledge acquisition among audiences who did not seek out the content for educational purposes, validating the entertainment-first learning model Salinlahi adopts. | Learning remains passive and observation-based. Viewers absorb historical context by following the story, but are never required to produce, recall, or apply any historical or cultural knowledge to continue watching. |
| The cross-cultural appeal of the drama among international audiences demonstrates that a compelling narrative can create investment in an unfamiliar cultural context, a motivational dynamic that Salinlahi applies to Baybayin for Filipino players. | As a broadcast television drama, Mr. Sunshine delivers cultural content at a fixed pace determined by its narrative structure. Viewers have no control over how deeply or quickly cultural content is introduced, and no mechanism to revisit or practice specific knowledge. |
| The production's historical research and collaboration with period consultants demonstrates that high entertainment value and cultural authenticity are achievable together. | The cultural learning effect is incidental and non-transferable in a structured sense. A viewer may emerge from the series with a stronger emotional connection to Korean history without being able to articulate specific cultural practices in a form that constitutes durable practical knowledge. |

## 

## **2.3 Synthesis** {#2.3-synthesis}

The literature and application analysis in this chapter converges on a coherent set of conditions that existing tools fail to satisfy and that Salinlahi's design is specifically constructed to address. The studies reviewed identify three overlapping failure modes. First, most tools rely on passive recognition tasks, multiple-choice quizzes and character matching that do not activate the motor encoding channels Fernandes et al. (2018) identify as the primary driver of durable character familiarity. Second, none of the tools reviewed place drawing under real-time gameplay pressure. Okami's brush mechanic is the most direct creative precedent but draws in a paused, time-free environment; Aralin Baybayin accepts freehand input but presents it within an explicitly instructional context. Magic Touch demonstrates that drawing under time pressure is both technically feasible and commercially viable, but applies it to invented symbols with no transferable value. Third, no existing tool applies on-device gesture recognition to process player-drawn characters as live functional game inputs at the millisecond latencies that gameplay requires.

Salinlahi's design responds to each of these gaps through a specific structural commitment. The intrinsic integration commitment, grounded in Habgood and Ainsworth (2011), ensures that drawing a Baybayin character is not a task that precedes or rewards gameplay, it is the gameplay. The $P algorithm commitment ensures that this drawing is processed in real time, offline, and without imposing stroke-order constraints that would penalize learners unfamiliar with historical scribal conventions. And the entertainment-first commitment, supported by the incidental learning precedents in Leon et al. (2025), Shintaku (2022), and Bacani and Hatami (2024), positions character familiarity as a natural byproduct of motivated gameplay rather than the game's stated purpose. Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller, 1988\) provides the unifying explanation for why this convergence works: when the learning action and the play action are the same action, the cognitive resources directed toward one are fully and simultaneously directed toward the other.

The application analysis also clarifies what Salinlahi inherits from existing work and what it does not. From Magic Touch it inherits the proof of concept that a single-mechanic drawing defense game can achieve mass-market engagement. From Okami it inherits the demonstration that drawing can be narratively meaningful and mechanically central. From Aralin Baybayin it inherits the confirmation that freehand Baybayin character input is technically feasible. From Golden Kamuy and Mr. Sunshine it inherits the evidence that entertainment-first cultural framing can produce real behavioral and knowledge outcomes in audiences who never sought instruction. What none of these precedents provide is the specific configuration that Salinlahi requires: a game in which a real cultural script, processed by an on-device recognizer, is the sole input mechanism under continuous real-time gameplay pressure. Table 7 summarizes this positioning across the key design dimensions the literature identifies as the conditions for effective intrinsically integrated, motor-encoding-driven, entertainment-first learning.

**Table 7\.**  
*Feature Comparison of Related Applications and Salinlahi Across Key Design Dimensions*

(Green \= present; Orange \= absent; White \= partially present or not applicable)
