| Feature | Learn Baybayin | Aralin Baybayin | Okami | Magic Touch | Golden Kamuy / Mr. Sun. | Salinlahi |
| ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Intrinsic integration (learning \= playing) | No | No | Partial | No | No | Yes |
| Freehand drawing as primary input | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| Drawing under real-time gameplay pressure | No | No | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| On-device character recognition | No | No | No | No | No | Yes |
| Uses a real cultural script | Yes | Yes | No | No | No | Yes |
| Mobile-native touchscreen design | Yes | Partial | No | Yes | N/A | Yes |
| Entertainment-first orientation | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Motor encoding through drawing production | No | Yes | Partial | No | No | Yes |
| Simultaneous multimodal feedback (drawing \+ audio \+ visual) | No | No | No | No | No | Yes |

# 

# **Chapter 3** {#chapter-3}

**Methodology**

## **3.1 Business Objectives** {#3.1-business-objectives}

1. The game should deliver an entertainment-first mobile experience where Baybayin character drawing is the sole combat mechanic, ensuring that playing the game and practicing the script are the same inseparable activity.  
2. The game should process freehand Baybayin character drawings as real-time combat inputs using the $P Point-Cloud Recognizer, validating drawn strokes on-device within milliseconds without requiring an internet connection.
3. The game should progressively introduce Baybayin characters across three game levels, giving players repeated in-game exposure to each character before the next set is added.  
4. The game should provide immediate audio and visual feedback on every drawing attempt, playing a pronunciation clip on success and a redraw indicator on failure, so the player always knows the outcome of their input.  
5. The game should be released as two separate applications: Salinlahi Lite, a free download that gives players access to the first three story mode levels, and Salinlahi, the full game available as a one-time purchase at PHP 149.00. Both versions should be available on the Google Play Store and the Apple App Store, with no advertisements, no in-app purchases, and no subscription fees in either version.  
6. The game should run stably on a broad range of Android and iOS mobile hardware, maintaining consistent frame rate and input responsiveness across the full game session without crashes.  
7. The game should score at or above the acceptable threshold on the System Usability Scale and produce positive scores on the Game Experience Questionnaire core module when evaluated by participants within the 13 to 25 target age range.

## **3.2 Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** {#3.2-software-development-life-cycle-(sdlc)}

The team uses the Agile Scrum Methodology. Since this project combines many types of technology, a traditional linear approach is not flexible enough. Scrum allows the team to adjust the plan whenever new technical issues arise. The development workflow consists of the following:

### **3.2.1 Scrum Roles** {#3.2.1-scrum-roles}

* **The Scrum Master:** One researcher will assume the role of the "Scrum Master." Their responsibility is to facilitate the process. They will remove "blockers" (e.g., resolving a git merge conflict) and ensure the team sticks to the schedule. They protect the development team from distractions.  
* **The Product Owner:** Another researcher will act as the "Product Owner." They represent the voice of the user. They manage the "Product Backlog," prioritizing which features are most important to build next based on the survey data.  
* **The Development Team:** The researchers collectively act as the developers, responsible for writing the code.

  ### **3.2.2 Scrum Artifacts and Events** {#3.2.2-scrum-artifacts-and-events}

* **The Sprints:** Work will be divided into strict two-week timeboxes called "Sprints." The goal of each Sprint is to produce a "Potentially Shippable Product Increment," a version of the software that actually works, even if it has limited features.  
* **Sprint Planning:** At the start of each cycle, the team will move tasks from the "Product Backlog" (prioritized list of work) to the "Sprint Backlog" (plan for the current sprint).  
* **Daily Stand-up Meeting:** A mandatory 15-minute meeting held every morning. Each member answers three questions: *What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? Is there anything blocking me?*  
* **Sprint Review & Retrospective:** At the end of the two weeks, the team demonstrates the working software to their adviser or consultants. They then hold a "Retrospective" to discuss what went well and what processes need to be improved for the next Sprint.

## **3.3 Technical Architecture and Tools** {#3.3-technical-architecture-and-tools}

This section details the technical architecture, software design patterns, and development tools that the team will use to build Salinlahi. The team focuses on proactive solutions to common game development problems, such as memory management and merge conflicts, to ensure the game runs smoothly on mobile devices. The entire game is built using Unity 6 LTS, which provides long term support and stability for mobile deployment.

### **3.3.1 Core Game Engine and Graphics Pipeline** {#3.3.1-core-game-engine-and-graphics-pipeline}

Unity 6 LTS is the primary development environment. The LTS release channel is chosen for its stable, long-term-supported codebase, which reduces the risk of breaking changes mid-development. The game uses Unity's Universal Render Pipeline (URP) configured for 2D, which provides a lighter rendering path on mobile hardware compared to the legacy built-in pipeline. No 3D geometry is used anywhere in the project.

The project is version-controlled with Git on GitHub. To reduce scene-merge conflicts, the team splits content across additive scenes where possible so team members rarely edit the same file simultaneously. Asset serialization is set to Force Text in Unity project settings so scene and prefab files are stored in human-readable YAML, which Git can diff and merge more reliably than binary formats.

### **3.3.2 Touch Input Capture System** {#3.3.2-touch-input-capture-system}

The game uses Unity's new Input System package, specifically the EnhancedTouch API, instead of the legacy Input.touches array. The legacy system polls touch data once per rendered frame, which means fast strokes can complete entirely within a frame gap and go unregistered. The EnhancedTouch API captures touch events at the device's input polling rate independently of the frame rate, so fast strokes are never missed.

The input manager subscribes to three touch phases: Began, which starts a new empty coordinate list; Moved, which appends the current screen position to the list on each callback; and Ended, which closes the list and sends it to the recognition module. Input capture and recognition are fully separated so both can be tested independently.

### **3.3.3 The Stroke Recognition Algorithm** {#3.3.3-the-stroke-recognition-algorithm}

The team implements the $P Point-Cloud Recognizer. $P treats a drawing as an unordered cloud of points rather than an ordered stroke sequence, which means it recognizes a correctly shaped character regardless of the order or direction the player drew it. This is a practical requirement for a game introducing Baybayin to players who have no prior knowledge of historical stroke conventions.

The algorithm normalizes each drawing in four steps: resampling to exactly 32 evenly spaced points (removing speed dependency), scaling to a 250x250 unit square (removing size dependency), translating the centroid to the origin (removing position dependency), and matching the point cloud against all 17 stored Baybayin character templates using a greedy Hungarian algorithm approximation to find the minimum total pairwise distance. The template with the lowest distance score wins. A confidence score at or above 0.60 is required for the recognition to be accepted.

Templates are stored as plain text coordinate files and loaded at startup, so they can be revised without recompiling the project. The full recognition pipeline, from finger lift to combat outcome, is expected to complete in under 50 milliseconds on target hardware.

### **3.3.4 Audio Feedback System** {#3.3.4-audio-feedback-system}

Audio feedback is a core part of the game's learning mechanism. Every time a player successfully draws a character, a voice pronunciation clip for that Baybayin syllable plays immediately. The timing of this clip is intentional: the sound fires at the exact moment the enemy is defeated, pairing the positive combat reward with the phonetic identity of the character the player just drew.

Each Baybayin characters has a corresponding audio clip stored as a Unity AudioClip asset. A dictionary maps each character identifier to its clip, which is populated at startup. When the recognition module returns a successful match, the character identifier is passed to the audio manager, which retrieves and plays the corresponding clip via an AudioSource component. Clips are kept short to avoid overlapping with the next enemy's appearance. All audio assets are bundled with the application and play fully offline.

### 	**3.3.5 Enemy Object Pooling** {#3.3.5-enemy-object-pooling}

Enemy waves repeatedly spawn and remove GameObjects throughout a session. On mobile hardware, frequent calls to Instantiate and Destroy trigger garbage collection, which causes frame rate hitches that are noticeable during fast-paced gameplay. To prevent this, the team uses Unity's built-in ObjectPool\<T\> class, available in Unity 6, to implement object pooling for enemy GameObjects.

At scene load, a fixed pool of enemy objects is pre-instantiated and held inactive. When a wave spawns an enemy, the Wave Manager retrieves an object from the pool and activates it with the appropriate character assignment and movement configuration. When an enemy is defeated or reaches the base, it is deactivated and returned to the pool rather than destroyed. This eliminates runtime allocation costs during wave gameplay and keeps frame rate consistent across long sessions, which directly supports the business objective of stable performance on a broad range of mobile hardware.

## **3.4 Business Model** {#3.4-business-model}

Salinlahi is distributed across two separate applications on the Google Play Store (Android) and the Apple App Store (iOS). The first is Salinlahi Lite, a free download that provides players with access to the first three story mode levels at no cost. The second is Salinlahi, the full game, priced at a one-time purchase of PHP 149.00. A single payment provides the player with complete and permanent access to everything the full application contains: the complete story mode chapter roster, the endless gameplay mode, the Chapter 1 boss battle, all cosmetic content, and all future content updates distributed within the same application version. There are no advertisements, no in-app purchases, no subscription fees, and no content locked behind additional payments of any kind in either version.

The two-application model is structured so that Salinlahi Lite functions as a playable entry point rather than a teaser or advertisement. Three full story mode levels give a new player enough time to learn how the drawing input works, encounter a meaningful range of Baybayin characters, and experience the pressure of wave-based gameplay before making a purchasing decision. Players who complete the Lite version and want to continue are directed to the full game's store listing. Both applications are built from the same Unity codebase. The distinction between them is managed through a build configuration flag that caps level access in the Lite build. This approach keeps the two versions consistent in quality and avoids duplicating development effort.

Salinlahi Lite clearly communicates its scope to the player through the main menu, where levels beyond the third are visually marked as unavailable in the Lite version. When the player completes Level 3, the win screen redirects to a content boundary screen rather than a Continue button. This screen informs the player that they have reached the end of the Lite version and provides a direct link to the full game's store listing. The boundary is presented as a natural conclusion to the trial experience rather than an interruption, which keeps the player's experience within the Lite version unbroken up to that point.

This model is a deliberate departure from the free-to-play (F2P) conventions that dominate the mobile gaming market. The decision rests on three interconnected arguments. First, Salinlahi's core design philosophy, grounded in the Intrinsic Integration Framework, requires that the player's experience remain uninterrupted and internally motivated at all times. Advertising interruptions, energy timers, and monetization prompts are structurally incompatible with a game whose educational effectiveness depends on sustained, unbroken engagement with the drawing mechanic. The Lite model eliminates this conflict entirely: neither version contains ads or interruptions, and the only point at which a purchase is ever mentioned is at the natural end of the Lite version's content. Second, the full game still establishes a clear value proposition: the player pays once for a complete cultural experience, which frames Salinlahi as a finished product rather than a service. The Lite version removes the risk of paying for something unknown, while the full purchase signals that the player found the experience worth continuing. This positioning is consistent with the game's mission to present Baybayin as something worth owning, not just something worth sampling. Third, the PHP 149.00 price point is calibrated to be accessible within the Philippine mobile market while remaining commercially viable for a student development team, as detailed in Section 3.5.

Salinlahi targets three primary user groups: Filipino youth aged 13 to 25 who play mobile games regularly and are seeking an engaging, culturally grounded experience; students and learners who wish to develop genuine familiarity with the Baybayin script in a voluntary, non-academic context; and cultural advocates, educators, and community organizations who may recommend or distribute the application as a cultural literacy resource. The game addresses the validated gap identified in the pre-development survey, where 90.0% of respondents expressed openness to learning Baybayin and 43.7% reported having previously attempted to learn it through existing tools before abandoning the effort.

Distribution is conducted exclusively through the Google Play Store and Apple App Store, the two platforms through which 82.1% of the target audience already access mobile content on a near-daily basis. Both Salinlahi Lite and Salinlahi maintain separate store listings, each with its own metadata, screenshots, and app identifier. App Store Optimization (ASO), including localized metadata, keyword targeting, and curated screenshots, serves as the primary discovery mechanism within each storefront. Supplementary channels include social media outreach through TikTok, Facebook, and Instagram, as well as direct school and university partnerships that provide institutional access to the target demographic through endorsement and on-site demonstration events.

Key operational activities include iterative content development and bug maintenance, digital marketing and community management, user support, and periodic user acceptance testing with partner institutions. To sustain cultural authenticity across future story chapters, the development team intends to engage Baybayin scholars, cultural historians, and language educators as consultants during content design phases.

## **3.5 Methods in Addressing Specific Objectives** {#3.5-methods-in-addressing-specific-objectives}

### **3.5.1 Objective 1: Entertainment-Focused Mobile Defense Game with Intrinsic Integration** {#3.5.1-objective-1:-entertainment-focused-mobile-defense-game-with-intrinsic-integration}

Objective: To design, develop, and evaluate an entertainment-focused mobile defense game where drawing characters is the core gameplay mechanic, not a separate learning activity, built around the Intrinsic Integration Framework.

The game is built as a portrait-mode vertical defense game. Enemies spawn at the top of the screen and move downward toward the player. The only way to defeat an enemy is to draw the Baybayin character displayed on it. No buttons, taps, or shortcuts exist. This constraint is enforced at the design level: no other input type is wired to any combat outcome. A player who does not draw correctly cannot progress.

Enemy waves are driven by a Wave Manager script that reads configuration data from Unity scriptable objects. Each level's wave roster, spawn delays, enemy types, and character assignments are stored in these data assets rather than in code. This separates content design from game logic, allowing the team to tune level difficulty without touching compiled scripts. Three levels are implemented with progressively larger character pools, faster spawn rates, and higher enemy movement speeds.

To evaluate if the game is truly entertainment-focused, the team will use the Game Experience Questionnaire core module during user testing. This will measure player enjoyment and immersion to confirm that the intrinsic integration framework successfully made the game fun.

### **3.5.2 Objective 2: Touch-Based Freehand Drawing as Primary Real-Time Input** {#3.5.2-objective-2:-touch-based-freehand-drawing-as-primary-real-time-input}

Objective: To determine whether combining freehand character drawing, audio pronunciation, and visual character display as a single live gameplay mechanic can build natural familiarity with the script without the player's deliberate intent to study.

Touch input is captured using Unity's EnhancedTouch API, which records stroke coordinates at the device's input polling rate rather than the render frame rate. This prevents fast strokes from being dropped during frame rate dips, which would create a frustrating and unreliable input experience under the time pressure of wave gameplay. To handle multi-stroke Baybayin characters, the input manager maintains a running list of completed strokes per character attempt. After each TouchPhase.Ended event, a coroutine-based idle timer begins. If a new stroke starts before the timer expires, the timer resets and the new stroke is appended to the current character's stroke collection. If the timer expires without a new touch input, the full stroke list is passed to the recognition module as a single character attempt. This approach accommodates both single-stroke and multi-stroke Baybayin characters without requiring the system to know the stroke count in advance.

Responsiveness is evaluated in two ways. During internal testing, the team measures the time from finger lift to displayed combat outcome across multiple devices and confirms it remains under 50 milliseconds. During user acceptance testing, participants complete the System Usability Scale (SUS) after playing through the first two levels. The SUS score is used to assess whether the drawing input feels natural and usable under real gameplay conditions. Open-ended post-play questions capture qualitative feedback on specific input pain points, such as strokes near screen edges or ambiguous character pairs.

To address the familiarity part of the objective, the game will silently log the player's drawing success rate in the background. By comparing the number of failed drawing attempts in Level 1 against the number of failed attempts in Level 3, the team can quantitatively measure if the player is naturally getting familiar with the Baybayin characters without explicitly studying them.

### **3.5.3 Objective 3: On-Device Baybayin Handwriting Recognition System** {#3.5.3-objective-3:-on-device-baybayin-handwriting-recognition-system}

Objective: To assess whether an on-device Baybayin handwriting recognition system can accurately and responsively process player-drawn characters under the real-time pressure of wave-based defense gameplay.

Recognition is handled by the $P Point-Cloud Recognizer implemented natively in Unity. The algorithm runs entirely on-device with no network dependency. Reference templates for all Baybayin characters are stored as plain text files and loaded at startup. The confidence threshold is set at 0.60, which is tuned to balance sensitivity against false positives on visually similar character pairs.

To formally assess the accuracy of the system under real-time pressure, the developers will implement a background logging system during playtesting. Every time a player submits a drawing, the game will record the target character, the drawing speed, and whether the algorithm accepted or rejected the input. After testing, the team will review these logs alongside player feedback to calculate the false positive and false negative rates. This will prove if the 0.60 threshold and the $P algorithm can reliably handle fast and panicked drawings during intense enemy waves.

## 

## **3.6 Survey / Interview Summary and Market Validation** {#3.6-survey-/-interview-summary-and-market-validation}

This section presents the results of a pre-development survey conducted to validate the market need for Salinlahi and to assess the target audience's relationship with Baybayin, mobile gaming, and voluntary cultural learning. A total of 341 respondents participated in the survey, of whom 337 (98.8%) fell within the primary target demographic of 13 to 25 years old. The data was collected from students across secondary and tertiary institutions.

### **3.6.1 Respondent Profile** {#3.6.1-respondent-profile}

The survey was answered by 341 respondents. The overwhelming majority, 337 respondents (98.8%) fall within the 13 to 25 age range that constitutes Salinlahi's primary target audience, with 181 respondents (53.1%) in the 13–18 bracket and 156 (45.7%) in the 19–25 bracket. Only a few respondents fell outside this range. This distribution provides strong demographic alignment between the survey sample and the intended player base.

**Figure 1\.**  
*Age Distribution of survey respondents (n=341)*  
![Forms response chart. Question title: Age Group. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image2]

### **3.6.2 Baybayin Awareness and Familiarity** {#3.6.2-baybayin-awareness-and-familiarity}

The data confirms a widespread awareness-literacy gap that directly motivates the development of Salinlahi. When asked about their familiarity with Baybayin, 206 respondents (60.4%) reported that they recognize some characters but cannot read or write the script, while 123 respondents (36.1%) have heard of it but cannot recognize any characters at all. Only 7 respondents (2.1%) reported the ability to read and write Baybayin with confidence, and 5 respondents (1.5%) had never heard of the script before the survey. In total, 96.8% of respondents lack functional literacy in Baybayin despite the overwhelming majority being aware of its existence.

**Figure 2\.**  
*Respondents’ familiarity with the Baybayin script (n=341)*  
![Forms response chart. Question title: How familiar are you with Baybayin, the pre-colonial writing script of the Philippines?. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image3]

This finding validates the central premise of the study. The problem Salinlahi addresses is not a lack of awareness, it is the failure of existing tools to convert awareness into practical familiarity.

### **3.6.3 Perceived Cultural Significance of Baybayin** {#3.6.3-perceived-cultural-significance-of-baybayin}

Respondents were asked to rate the cultural significance of Baybayin to Filipino identity on a scale of 1 to 5\. The results demonstrate that Filipino youth hold Baybayin in high cultural regard: 208 respondents (61.0%) gave the script the highest significance rating of 5, and an additional 95 respondents (27.9%) rated it a 4\. Cumulatively, 88.9% of respondents rated Baybayin's cultural significance at 4 or 5, indicating that the target population recognizes the script's importance to their heritage. This pre-existing sense of cultural value represents a favorable motivational environment for Salinlahi, as the game does not need to build the case for Baybayin's relevance because the audience already believes in it.

**Figure 3\.**  
*Respondents' ratings of Baybayin's cultural significance to Filipino identity (n \= 341\)*  
***![Forms response chart. Question title: Regardless of your familiarity with it, how significant do you feel Baybayin is to Filipino cultural identity?. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image4]***

### 

### **3.6.4 Prior Learning Attempts and the Dropout Problem** {#3.6.4-prior-learning-attempts-and-the-dropout-problem}

Respondents were asked whether they had ever actively tried to learn Baybayin. The results reveal a significant dropout problem that existing tools have failed to solve: 149 respondents (43.7%) reported that they had tried to learn Baybayin but stopped before feeling confident. A further 158 respondents (46.3%) had never attempted to learn it but expressed openness to trying. Only 14 respondents (4.1%) reported successfully learning Baybayin with confidence, while 20 respondents (5.9%) expressed no interest in learning it.

**Figure 4\.**  
*Respondents' prior attempts to learn Baybayin (n \= 341\)*  
![Forms response chart. Question title: Have you ever actively tried to learn Baybayin through any resource?. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image5]

The finding that 43.7% of respondents attempted to learn Baybayin and abandoned the effort before achieving confidence is the most direct empirical validation of the problem Salinlahi is designed to solve. The existing tools are not failing at awareness as they are failing at retention. When combined with the 46.3% who are open to trying, Salinlahi's total addressable audience among respondents who are either curious or previously motivated represents 90.0% of the sample.

### **3.6.5 Motivation: School Requirement Versus Embedded Game Mechanic** {#3.6.5-motivation:-school-requirement-versus-embedded-game-mechanic}

To directly test the motivational premise of Salinlahi's design, respondents were asked to rate their motivation to learn Baybayin under two distinct conditions on a scale of 1 to 5: first, if it were required as a graded school subject; and second, if it were embedded in a mobile game they genuinely enjoyed.

Under the school-requirement condition, 179 respondents (52.5%) gave the highest motivation rating of 5, with a mean weighted toward the upper end of the scale. Under the game-embedded condition, 192 respondents (56.3%) gave the highest rating of 5, with only 2 respondents (0.6%) selecting the lowest motivation rating of 1, compared to 9 respondents (2.6%) under the school condition. The game-embedded condition produced higher ratings at the top of the scale and lower ratings at the bottom, indicating that embedding Baybayin in enjoyable gameplay is at least as motivating as formal academic requirement and in distributional terms, slightly more so. More importantly, the game condition produced stronger engagement at the extremes: more respondents rated it a 5, and fewer rated it a 1, compared to the school condition.

**Table 7\.**  
*Comparison of learning motivation under school-requirement vs. game-embedded conditions (n \= 341\)*

| Motivation Rating | School (n) | School (%) | Game (n) | Game (%) |
| ----- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 5 — Highest motivation | 179 | 52.5% | 192 | 56.3% |
| 4 | 95 | 27.9% | 106 | 31.1% |
| 3 | 43 | 12.6% | 31 | 9.1% |
| 2 | 15 | 4.4% | 10 | 2.9% |
| 1 — Lowest motivation | 9 | 2.6% | 2 | 0.6% |

These results provide direct empirical support for the core design argument of Salinlahi: that embedding Baybayin learning in an engaging game mechanic is at least as effective a motivational lever as formal academic requirement, without the coercive structure that academic requirements entail.

### **3.6.6 Mobile Gaming Habits of the Target Audience** {#3.6.6-mobile-gaming-habits-of-the-target-audience}

These results provide direct empirical support for the core design argument of Salinlahi: that embedding Baybayin learning in an engaging game mechanic is at least as effective a motivational lever as formal academic requirement, without the coercive structure that academic requirements entail.

**Figure 5\.**  
*Frequency of mobile game play among survey respondents (n \= 341\)*  
***![Forms response chart. Question title: How often do you play mobile games?. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image6]***

This distribution confirms that the target audience is already engaged with the medium in which Salinlahi is designed. The game does not need to build a habit of mobile gaming in its audience, it needs only to make its content compelling enough to occupy time that respondents are already spending on their mobile devices.

### **3.6.7 Incidental Learning Through Entertainment: Established Precedent** {#3.6.7-incidental-learning-through-entertainment:-established-precedent}

The survey also investigated the mechanisms through which Filipino youth have already experienced incidental learning — the acquisition of knowledge as a byproduct of engaging in an enjoyable activity rather than through deliberate study. When asked how they learned the thing they identify as having learned well outside of school, 155 respondents (45.5%) reported that the learning happened naturally while they were doing something they enjoyed, and 159 respondents (46.6%) reported a mix of intentional and incidental learning. Only 9 respondents (2.6%) reported purely deliberate study as their learning pathway.

**Figure 6\.**  
*Respondents' primary mode of informal learning outside school (n \= 341)*![Forms response chart. Question title: For what you identified in the previous question, did you intend to learn it, or did the learning happen as a side effect of doing something enjoyable? (skip if not applicable). Number of responses: 330 responses.][image7]

These findings align directly with the theoretical framework underpinning Salinlahi's design. The majority of the target audience has already experienced and responded positively to incidental learning through entertainment. The game is designed to reproduce this mechanism for Baybayin acquisition.

### **3.6.8 The Entertainment-Driven Cultural Learning Precedent** {#3.6.8-the-entertainment-driven-cultural-learning-precedent}

When asked why they know a foreign script (such as Japanese Katakana or Korean Hangul) better than Baybayin, 257 respondents (75.4%) attributed their foreign script familiarity to the fact that it was tied to entertainment they already enjoyed, such as anime, K-pop, or games. This is the most striking finding in the dataset. Three out of four respondents have already demonstrated that entertainment-mediated learning can produce cultural and linguistic acquisition, specifically the kind of incidental acquisition that Salinlahi is designed to replicate for Filipino heritage content.

**Figure 7\.**  
*Respondents' reasons for knowing a foreign script better than Baybayin (n \= 341)*![Forms response chart. Question title: If you know a foreign script better than Baybayin (e.g., Japanese Katakana or Korean Hangul) what do you think is the main reason for that difference?. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image8]

This finding provides both the problem diagnosis and the solution validation in a single data point. The reason Filipino youth know Korean Hangul or Japanese Katakana better than their own ancestral script is not because foreign scripts are more valuable or more accessible and it is because they are entangled with entertainment that Filipino youth already consume voluntarily. Salinlahi applies this exact mechanism to Baybayin.

### **3.6.9 Established Track Record of Game-Mediated Learning** {#3.6.9-established-track-record-of-game-mediated-learning}

Respondents were asked whether they had ever learned something genuinely useful like a word in another language, a historical fact, a practical skill through a game. 233 respondents (68.3%) confirmed that this had happened to them, and an additional 105 respondents (30.8%) believed it had, though they were not certain. Combined, 99.1% of respondents either confirmed or suspected that games had taught them something of genuine value outside the game context. Only 2 respondents (0.6%) explicitly stated they did not believe games had taught them anything real.

**Figure 8\.**  
*Respondents' experience of learning something genuinely useful through a game (n \= 341\)*  
***![Forms response chart. Question title: Have you ever learned something genuinely useful such as a word in another language, a historical fact, or a real skill while playing a game, without the game explicitly telling you it was teaching you?. Number of responses: 341 responses.][image9]***

This near-universal endorsement of game-based incidental learning among the target demographic provides strong behavioral validation for the premise that a well-designed game can produce genuine knowledge transfer. The skeptical argument that games cannot be serious learning tools is not a position held by the target audience.

### **3.6.10 Open-Ended Responses: Demand Signal for Salinlahi's Design** {#3.6.10-open-ended-responses:-demand-signal-for-salinlahi's-design}

The survey's open-ended question asked respondents to describe what a tool would need to offer for them to voluntarily and consistently use it to learn Baybayin. Analysis of the free-text responses reveals recurring themes that converge directly on Salinlahi's design.

The dominant response pattern was a demand for a game-based experience in which Baybayin functions as a mechanic rather than a subject. Respondents described wanting a tool where "Baybayin just happens to be how you interact with it," where "the main point is that it is fun," and where "the learning is inside the gameplay and not announced." Several respondents described the ideal tool in terms that precisely match Salinlahi's design: a defense game where drawing Baybayin characters correctly is the only action available to the player. One respondent stated: "I need a system where the learning is structurally unavoidable. Not optional, not attached on the side, but built into the only action I have." Another described: "Good design and actual stakes. If enemies are coming at my base and the only way to stop them is drawing the right Baybayin character before they arrive, that is fun."

A second dominant theme was explicit rejection of the instructional model. Respondents consistently described disengaging from tools that "feel like studying," that "explicitly say \[character\] stands for \[syllable\]," or that are designed in a way "my teacher would recommend." This pattern of resistance to academic framing validates the design principle that Salinlahi must not announce itself as an educational tool.

A third theme was the importance of active production over passive recognition. Respondents described wanting to draw and make, not just look and select. "I retain things when I use them, not when I study them" was a representative formulation. This preference aligns with the cognitive research on drawing as a memory strategy that underpins Salinlahi's touch-based character-drawing mechanic.

The convergence of these open-ended responses with Salinlahi's design specification is strong evidence of product-market fit. The audience is not merely open to a game-based Baybayin tool, in their own words, a game in which Baybayin is the mechanic, not the subject, is precisely what they describe wanting.

### 

### **3.6.11 Market Validation Summary** {#3.6.11-market-validation-summary}

The survey data collectively validates the core market assumptions underlying Salinlahi's development across five dimensions:

1. Demand exists. 90.0% of respondents are either open to learning Baybayin or have previously attempted to learn it. The market is not apathetic toward the product's subject matter.  
2. Existing tools are failing. 43.7% of respondents tried to learn Baybayin through existing resources and stopped before achieving confidence. The product gap is not a lack of awareness; it is a failure of retention by available tools.  
3. The target audience is a mobile gaming audience. 82.1% of respondents play mobile games at least three times per week. The distribution channel and engagement model are well-matched to the audience's existing behavior.  
4. Entertainment-driven learning is a proven pathway for this audience. 75.4% of respondents have already demonstrated the capacity for entertainment-mediated cultural acquisition by learning a foreign script through anime, K-pop, or games. Salinlahi applies the same mechanism to Filipino heritage content.  
5. The audience's own words describe Salinlahi. Open-ended responses from a significant portion of the sample describe an ideal learning tool that is structurally identical to Salinlahi's design: a game in which Baybayin is the mechanic, active drawing is the primary interaction, and the educational content is inseparable from the gameplay.

   ### **3.6.12 Expert Interview Summary** {#3.6.12-expert-interview-summary}

   To supplement the quantitative survey findings with qualitative depth, the research team conducted semi-structured interviews with two informants selected for their demonstrated familiarity with the Baybayin script: a fourth-year Bachelor of Arts in English Language Studies student with self-reported functional Baybayin literacy acquired through independent study, and a second respondent with substantive engagement in Baybayin pedagogy and script history. Both interviews were conducted during the pre-development phase.

   Three findings from these interviews are directly relevant to Salinlahi's design rationale.

   First, both informants independently confirmed the application gap as the primary mechanism behind learner dropout. The second respondent characterized the problem in terms that directly parallel the survey's 43.7% dropout finding: without daily contexts in which the script can be read, written, or used functionally, familiarity degrades regardless of initial motivation. Existing tools produce exposure but not the sustained, repeated engagement that retention requires. Salinlahi addresses this by manufacturing repeated, pressure-driven engagement through the wave defense mechanic. The player encounters each character dozens of times across a session not because they are studying it but because the game requires it for survival.

   Second, the second respondent identified the spelling fallacy as the most persistent cognitive error among new learners: the tendency, conditioned by Roman alphabet literacy, to decompose Baybayin syllables into individual letters and treat each letter as a separate character input. This error is structurally unreachable by passive recognition tools, which present characters in isolation. It is directly addressed by Salinlahi's freehand drawing mechanic, in which the player produces each character as a single unified gesture corresponding to a complete syllabic unit, which is the correct cognitive model for the script.

   Third, when asked whether Baybayin could be effectively taught through a game, the second respondent endorsed the approach directly, citing a Duolingo-style decoding mechanic as a workable model. Salinlahi's design differs from the Duolingo model in a structurally significant way: where Duolingo separates the learning task from the game reward, Salinlahi makes the drawing the only game action, such that the learning and the playing are the same activity. The expert's endorsement of gamification as a valid medium therefore supports Salinlahi's approach at minimum and likely undersells it.
